Notice Of Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration
Off Calendar May-15-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DANIEL COX, Esq. of Quill & Arrow, LLP (tel: 310-933-4271) for Plaintiff.
Atty. ALI AMEIRPOUR, Esq. of Theta Law Firm, LLP (tel: 424-297-3103) for Defendant Mercedes-Benz USA, LLC.
Atty. MADISON KING, Esq. of Kolar & Associates (tel: 714-544-0041) for Defendant AN San Jose Luxury Imports, INC.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MERCEDES-BENZ USA, LLC's Motion To Compel Binding Arbitration.

Matter discussed. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Mercedez-Benz USA, LLC's ("MBUSA") motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is denied.

Plaintiff Jimmy Nguyen purchased a vehicle from a dealer in 2021, executing a contract which included an agreement to arbitrate. Nguyen filed a complaint in September 2023 alleging statutory violations against MBUSA. MBUSA now files this motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings, alleging that it has standing to bring the motion under the doctrine of equitable estoppel and as a third-party beneficiary.

The court rejects MBUSA's equitable estoppel argument because Nguyen's claims are not "intimately founded in and intertwined' with the underlying contract obligations." (Yeh v. Superior Court of Contra Costa County (2023) 95 Cal.App.5th 264, 272; Ochoa v. Ford Motor Company (2023) 89 Cal.App.5th 1324, 1333; Kielar v. Superior Court (2023) 94 Cal.App.5th 614, 620; Montemayor v. Ford Motor Co. (2023) 92 Cal.App.5th 958, 971 (all disagreeing with Felisilda v. FCA US LLC (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 486).) Nguyen's complaint does not rely on the financing agreement. Nguyen is trying to enforce MBUSA's statutory obligations under the Song-Beverly Act to adequately repair, replace, or repurchase the car at issue. The court declines to stay the matter pending the California Supreme Court's review of Ochoa.

Additionally, MBUSA is not a third-party beneficiary of the arbitration agreement. MBUSA must show (1) "the third party would in fact benefit from the contract"; (2) "a motivating purpose of the contracting parties was to provide a benefit to the third party"; and (3) permitting the third party to enforce the contract "is consistent with the objectives of the contract and the reasonable expectations of the contracting parties." (Goonewardene v. ADP, LLC (2019) 6 Cal.5th 817, 830.) The contract here does not demonstrate an express intent to benefit MBUSA, nor would it be a reasonable expectation of the contracting parties that MBUSA would enforce the arbitration clause.

Because MBUSA does not have standing to bring the motion to compel arbitration, the motion is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter stating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
